evidence_quotes.quote: A game should not cease to work and be unplayable just because it was tied to a server.
put that functionality behind a subscription paywall.
This retroactive modification of the original purchase seems completely backwards when compared to all other purchases of goods; when you bought it, it's yours, and that should be it.

paragraphs.text: The act should address product functionality being removed or reduced due to a reliance on external services. Stop Killing Games already makes a good point about this as far as games are concerned. A game should not cease to work and be unplayable just because it was tied to a server. The game should continue to work offline, or there should be at least some possibility for the consumer to restore basic functionality of the game. A game that's designed to phone home back to a server and refuse to be playable without one, assuming that it's also not possible for a consumer to set up their own server, is designed in a user-hostile way and goes against basic digital fairness. The same thing does happen with actual products. Smart home products from companies like Futurehome should not suddenly stop working just because the company behind them push an update to remove functionality; Futurehome's products were fully functional offline when originally sold, but an update broke that functionality and instead put that functionality behind a subscription paywall. This retroactive modification of the original purchase seems completely backwards when compared to all other purchases of goods; when you bought it, it's yours, and that should be it. All functionality that was possible to do offline before should always be possible for the lifetime of that product.
